Greek & Spanish · 30–45 min/day

8-Week Greek Sprint

Check off weeks as you complete them

0Weeks Done
8Total Weeks
~6.5hPer Week

📅 Daily Session Structure

30 min
Language TransferYour daily anchor — 1–2 lessons of "Complete Greek" (~10–15 min each). Free, audio-only, builds grammar intuition. Pause and repeat as instructed.
10 min
Anki FlashcardsDue cards first, then 5–10 new topic-focused cards. Download "Greek 5000 Most Common Words".
+30 min
Pimsleur or GreekPod101On days with extra time. One full Pimsleur lesson (deep audio drill) or a GreekPod101 episode. Either reinforces what Language Transfer introduces.
Weekly
italki (2–3× per week)Month 1: introductions & survival → restaurants. Month 2: transport, relational, full immersion scenarios.

🎓 Daily Time on This Trainer

Beyond the Language Transfer / Anki anchor above — here's how to split your ~30 minutes/day inside the trainer itself. Adjust to match what's leaking that week.

15 min
🎧 Practice (Audio Lesson / Flashcard / Listen→Pick) The bulk of your trainer time. Run an Audio Lesson hands-free (EN→GR for production, GR→EN for comprehension). Filter by topic chip if you're targeting a leak (🚫 δεν reflex, 🗓️ Months, 🥤 πίνω). Mark hard cards with ⭐ so they surface in 🎯 My Mistakes.
10 min
🧠 Grammar Drill One or two grammar modes per session — pick whichever is leaking (Articles, Type A/B Verbs, Adjective Agreement, Gender Tap, Time Expressions, Negation). The Random button (🎲) auto-picks if you'd rather not decide.
5 min
📚 Rules review Skim the Macro Patterns and the May 14 Rule Codebook. You're not memorizing fresh — you're priming the patterns so they fire faster in conversation. Read the call-out boxes (the yellow ones) more carefully than the body text.
Weekly
🔍 Review tab + Drill Priorities Once a week (Sunday is a good anchor) open the Review tab, look at your mistake patterns, and read the 🎯 Drill Priorities card in Rules. Reset next week's focus based on what's actually leaking — not what feels productive.

📚 8-Week Curriculum

Week 0 — Setup …

System build · Tutor booked · Ambient exposure begins

Setup week Build the Sessions tab. Book italki trial session. Flash through alphabet in the Alphabet Drill tab. Flip phone OS → Greek. Greek music/podcast in background. No formal curriculum yet.
🎯 System ready · Tutor on calendar

Month 1 — Foundation & Survival

· Alphabet → Restaurants

Week 1 — Alphabet, Greetings & Intro Greek alphabet phonetically. Γεια σας, με λένε…, where you're from. First full italki session.
🎯 Read a menu phonetically
Week 2 — Numbers, Time & Survival Phrases Count to 100, tell time, please/thank you/excuse me, "I don't understand."
🎯 Handle price transactions
Week 3 — Restaurants: Ordering Menu reading, ordering food & drinks, asking what something is, dietary needs.
Week 4 — Restaurants: Connecting Paying, compliments, asking for recommendations.
🎯 Order full meal end-to-end

Month 2 — Connection & Polish

· Transport → Free Conversation

Week 5 — Directions & Transport "Where is…?", left/right/straight, metro/bus/taxi, handling being lost, airport phrases.
🎯 Navigate transport independently
Week 6 — About Yourself + Past Tense Basics Job, family, trip purpose. "I went / I saw / I liked." Shift from transactional to relational.
Week 7 — Talking to Locals + Follow-up Questions Their recommendations, opinions, life. Asking follow-ups. Compliments (πολύ ωραίο!).
🎯 Real 3-min conversation
Week 8 — Scenario Marathon + Free Conversation Drill all 20 scenarios. No new material. Full italki immersion.
🏆 Trip-ready!

🧠 Language Transfer — Complete Greek (Your Daily Anchor)

▶ Open Language Transfer ↗
120 lessons total · ~10–15 min each · audio-only · pause + repeat as instructed
0 of 120 lessons completed
Lessons 1–10
Foundations. μένω, περιμένω, ξέρω, θέλω, κάνω. Cognates from English (peri-, epi-). The "I" form (-ω) and "he/she/it" form (-ει).
Lessons 11–30
Pronouns + cognate nouns. εγώ, εσύ, αυτός/αυτή/αυτό, εμείς. Greek-derived English nouns (πρόβλημα, σύστημα, διάγραμμα, κλίμα). Possessives (μου, σου, του).
Lessons 31–60
Past tense + perfective verbs. Imperfective (ongoing) vs perfective (completed) verb stems — μάθω vs μαθαίνω, καταλάβω vs καταλαβαίνω.
Lessons 61–90
Future + conditional. "θα" + verb structure for future. Comparisons (πιο, πολύ). More complex sentences.
Lessons 91–120
Refinement + conversation. Idioms, complex sentences, fluency-level patterns. By here you can build sentences without thinking.

🎧 Pimsleur Greek Level 1 — Cheat Sheet

▶ Open Pimsleur App ↗
Updates which lessons are unlocked below
0 of 30 lessons completed

🗂 Vocabulary by Topic

🍽 Restaurant Essentials
ΠαρακαλώPlease / You're welcome
ΕυχαριστώThank you
Τον κατάλογοThe menu
Θα ήθελα…I would like…
Τον λογαριασμόThe bill
Πολύ νόστιμο!Very delicious!
🚇 Getting Around
Πού είναι…;Where is…?
Αριστερά / ΔεξιάLeft / Right
ΕυθείαStraight ahead
Πόσο κάνει;How much does it cost?
Σταμάτα εδώStop here (taxi)
Το αεροδρόμιοThe airport
👋 Greetings & Small Talk
Γεια σας / Γεια σουHello (formal/informal)
ΚαλημέραGood morning
ΚαληνύχταGood night
Με λένε…My name is…
Είμαι από…I'm from…
Μαθαίνω ελληνικάI'm learning Greek
🆘 Survival Phrases
Δεν καταλαβαίνωI don't understand
Πιο αργά παρακαλώSlower please
Μιλάτε αγγλικά;Do you speak English?
Βοήθεια!Help!
ΣυγγνώμηSorry / Excuse me
ΧάθηκαI'm lost

🔗 Your Resource Stack

🎧
Pimsleur
Your daily anchor. 30-min audio lessons. Greek Level 1 & 2 = 50 lessons total.
Daily · Greek
🧠
Language Transfer
Free "Complete Greek" audio course by Mihalis Eleftheriou. ~120 lessons. Exceptional for building grammar intuition.
Daily · Free
🃏
Anki
Spaced repetition flashcards. Search "Greek 5000 Most Common Words" deck to start.
Daily · Both
💬
italki
1–2× weekly conversation sessions. Community tutors ~$8–15. Most important investment.
Weekly · Both
📻
GreekPod101
Situational audio/video lessons for shadowing practice. Free tier available.
Daily · Greek
▶️
Learn Greek with Lina
YouTube channel. Great for Weeks 6–8 listening practice and natural conversation patterns.
Weeks 6–8 · Greek
🗣
Google Translate Voice
Safety net only — not for studying. Use when completely stuck in a real situation.
Backup · Both

Spanish Learning Plan

Full plan ready — start after Greece or run in parallel

0Weeks Done
12Total Weeks
~5hPer Week

📅 Daily Session Structure

30 min
Pimsleur SpanishCore daily anchor. Spanish moves faster than Greek — you may skip ahead in lessons.
10 min
Anki FlashcardsSpanish 5000 Most Common Words deck. Huge content library available.
+15 min
SpanishPod101 / Netflix en EspañolMonth 2+: Spanish TV with Spanish subtitles is incredibly effective immersion.
Weekly
italki (1–2× per week)Massive tutor pool. Many community tutors under $10/hr. Rich resource.

📚 12-Week Curriculum

Month 1 — Foundation

Core Grammar · Essential Verbs · Numbers

Week 1 — Pronunciation & Basics Spanish phonetics are intuitive. Focus on rolling R, accent marks, and vowel sounds.
🎯 Introduce yourself fluently
Week 2 — Present Tense Verbs Ser vs. estar, tener, querer, ir. These unlock 80% of basic conversation.
Week 3 — Numbers, Time & Days Count, tell time, schedule, handle prices. Much easier than Greek!
Week 4 — Questions & Negation ¿Qué, cómo, dónde, cuándo, por qué? Build your full question toolkit.
🎯 Ask & answer 10 question types

Month 2 — Situational Fluency

Restaurants · Transport · Daily Life

Week 5 — Food & Ordering Restaurant vocabulary, ordering, dietary restrictions, paying the bill.
Week 6 — Getting Around Directions, transport, "¿Dónde está…?", taxi & metro phrases.
🎯 Navigate a city independently
Week 7 — Shopping & Haggling ¿Cuánto cuesta? Sizes, colors, "too expensive", bargaining confidently.
Week 8 — Past Tense Intro Preterite for key verbs. Talk about what you did, where you went.
🎯 Tell a simple story in past tense

Month 3 — Fluency Push

Small Talk · Idioms · Real Conversation

Week 9 — Talking About Yourself Work, hobbies, family, opinions. Me gusta, quiero, espero…
Week 10 — Idioms & Natural Speech Colloquial phrases that make you sound real, not textbook-perfect.
🎯 10-min free conversation
Week 11 — Future & Plans Voy a…, quiero…, planeo… Talk about your trip and future plans.
Week 12 — Full Immersion Week Phone in Spanish. Think in Spanish. All italki sessions Spanish only.
🏆 Conversationally fluent!

🗂 Vocabulary by Topic

🍽 Restaurant Essentials
Por favorPlease
GraciasThank you
La carta / El menúThe menu
Quisiera…I would like…
La cuentaThe bill
¡Está delicioso!It's delicious!
🚇 Getting Around
¿Dónde está…?Where is…?
A la izquierda / derechaLeft / Right
Todo rectoStraight ahead
¿Cuánto cuesta?How much is it?
Pare aquíStop here (taxi)
El aeropuertoThe airport
👋 Greetings & Small Talk
Hola / Buenos díasHi / Good morning
¿Cómo estás?How are you?
Me llamo…My name is…
Soy de…I'm from…
Mucho gustoNice to meet you
Estoy aprendiendo españolI'm learning Spanish
🆘 Survival Phrases
No entiendoI don't understand
Más despacio, por favorSlower please
¿Habla inglés?Do you speak English?
¡Ayuda!Help!
Perdón / DisculpeSorry / Excuse me
Estoy perdido/aI'm lost

👨 Practice with Dad

Your dad is one of your best resources. Use this section to prepare for sessions, track what you've covered, and log what you learn.

✅ Topics Practiced Together

Tap a topic to mark it as practiced with Dad.

○ Greetings
○ Introductions
○ Numbers
○ Restaurant
○ Directions
○ Transport
○ Shopping
○ Small Talk
○ Family & Home
○ Weather
○ Greek Culture
○ Pronunciation
○ Alphabet
○ Fun/Slang 😄
🎭 Role-Play Scenarios to Try
🍽
At a tavernaDad plays the waiter. Order a full meal, ask what's good, pay the bill.
🗺
Lost in AthensDad gives directions in Greek. You have to follow them and confirm you understood.
🛺
Hailing a taxiTell the driver where you're going, ask how long, negotiate or confirm price.
🛍
Market shoppingAsk for items, ask the price, say it's too expensive, try to haggle.
☕
Meeting a localSmall talk at a café — introduce yourself, ask about them, talk about your trip.
📞
Phone call practiceTry a full conversation on the phone — no body language, forces real listening.
💬 Conversation Starter Prompts
Ask Dad to describe his childhood home in Greek — practice listening and asking follow-up questions.
Have Dad say 5 random words and you guess the meaning. Switch and you say words, he corrects your pronunciation.
Ask Dad what phrases tourists always get wrong — insider corrections are gold.
Ask him to teach you one Greek proverb and explain it. Then try to use it in a sentence.
Ask Dad to read a simple Greek text out loud and you follow along, stopping him to ask about words you don't know.
Practice the hardest sounds in Greek (γ, χ, ρ) — have Dad drill you until they sound right.
Ask Dad: "How would you actually say this in real life?" — textbook Greek vs natural spoken Greek can differ a lot.

📝 Session Notes

✓ Saved!

Αα Greek Alphabet Drill

Three modes: browse the full reference table, drill with flashcards, or test yourself with the matching game.

📱
iPhone in Greek — pronunciation trainer ~80 high-frequency Greek words you'll see on your phone (Settings, apps, Maps, Messages), with pronunciation drills + recovery path. ~80 min full run, or jump in and out.
→

📐 Greek Articles — The "the" and "a" Paradigm

Articles change with gender (masc/fem/neut), number (sing/plural), AND case (nominative/accusative/genitive). Memorise this grid — it unlocks everything.

Definite ("the")

MascFemNeut
Sing Nomοητο
Sing Accτοντηντο
Sing Genτουτηςτου
Plur Nomοιοιτα
Plur Accτουςτιςτα
Plur Genτωντωντων

Indefinite ("a / an")

MascFemNeut
Nomέναςμίαένα
Accένανμίαένα
Genενόςμιαςενός

No plural — Greek uses bare nouns or μερικά (some) instead.

Survival rules: (1) Match the article's gender to the noun. (2) Use Nominative for the subject — "ο άντρας έρχεται" (the man comes). (3) Use Accusative for the direct object — "βλέπω τον άντρα" (I see the man). (4) Use Genitive for "of / from / 's" — "το σπίτι του άντρα" (the man's house). (5) When in doubt, say it anyway — locals hear it, infer it, and reply.

How to tell if a noun is masculine, feminine, or neuter

Greek noun endings predict gender most of the time. Learn these three patterns and you'll pick the right article without thinking.

Masculine — ο
Most -ος, -ας, -ης endings
ο άντρας · ο φίλος · ο κόσμος · ο δρόμος · ο μήνας · ο μπαμπάς
Feminine — η
Most -η, -α endings
η μαμά · η αδερφή · η μπύρα · η οικογένεια · η ψυχή · η μέρα
Neuter — το
Most -ο, -ι, -μα endings
το νερό · το κρασί · το παιδί · το πρόβλημα · το σύστημα · το σπίτι

Agreement: article + adjective + noun all change together

  • All three must match in gender, number, and case. Masc: ο μεγάλος άντρας. Fem: η μεγάλη γυναίκα. Neut: το μεγάλο σπίτι.
  • When the noun's case changes, article and adjective change with it. Subject: ο μεγάλος άντρας έρχεται (the big man comes). Object: βλέπω τον μεγάλο άντρα (I see the big man). Possessor: το σπίτι του μεγάλου άντρα (the big man's house).
  • Greek uses articles where English doesn't: with country names (η Ελλάδα είναι όμορφη — Greece is beautiful), with personal names (ο Γιάννης ξέρει — Yannis knows), and with abstract / general nouns (μου αρέσει το παγωτό — I like ice cream).
  • Greek drops indefinite articles where English uses them: είμαι Αμερικανός = "I am American" (no "an"). είναι γιατρός = "she is a doctor".
  • Common gotchas: a few -ι words are NOT neuter (rare), and a few -ος words are feminine (η οδός = the street, η νήσος = the island). When you meet a noun, learn it WITH its article — "ο φίλος", not just "φίλος".
Α ah (as in father) Alpha tap to reveal

  1. STRESS — final syllable loud? (μι-ΛΑ-ω, ο-δη-ΓΩ) → Group B (-ήσω). Stop.
  2. LIST — on the Top-10? → Irregular.
    βλέπω · λέω · τρώω · πίνω · βρίσκω · βάζω · παίρνω · δίνω · φεύγω · μαθαίνω
  3. TAIL — read the ending:
    -εύω → -εύω group · -ώνω → -ώνω group · other consonant+ω → Group A (letter swap)
Order matters. S → L → T. Each step rules out a class.

        Plan for: — Generated: —

        🎯 Why these picks

        —

        🎙️ Today's 5 speak prompts

          Switch to Speak to drill these. (Auto-loading these into Speak is a Phase 2 polish.)

          📚 Grammar focus

          📒 Vocab focus

            📚 Greek Rules & Endings

            Your running cheat sheet — the rules and endings worth memorizing. Glance here before any speaking practice.

            🔑 Key Rules to Remember
            να separates verbs
            When you have two verbs back-to-back, να links them — and the second verb takes its closed form.
            Θέλω να δω φωτογραφία — I want to see a photo (not βλέπω → δω)
            Θέλω να ξυπνήσω στις 8 — I want to wake up at 8 (not ξυπνάω → ξυπνήσω)
            δεν vs. όχι — two different "no"s
            δεν only goes with verbs. όχι goes with everything else (nouns, adverbs, adjectives).
            Δεν είναι εδώ — is not here (verb → δεν)
            Όχι σήμερα — not today (adverb → όχι)
            Double negative — always required
            Greek does not drop the δεν when you also have ποτέ / κανένας / τίποτα. Keep both.
            Ποτέ δεν πίνω καφέ — I never drink coffee
            Κανένας δεν είναι εδώ — No one is here
            Δεν θέλω τίποτα — I don't want anything
            Time expressions: τον/την/το vs. στις
            Time expressions take the article (τον/την/το) — except clock times and dates, which take στις.
            EnglishGreekRule
            on Mondayτην Δευτέραarticle
            in Julyτον Ιούλιοarticle
            in the afternoonτο απόγευμαarticle
            in 2026το 2026article
            at 9 o'clockστις εννέα η ώραclock → στις
            on July 15thστις δεκαπέντε Ιουλίουdate → στις
            Adjectives match the noun's gender
            The adjective ending changes to match the gender (and number) of the noun it describes.
            Η Ναταλία είναι κουρασμένη — fem. noun → fem. adjective ending -η
            Ο Νίκος είναι κουρασμένος — masc. noun → masc. ending -ος
            Open vs. closed verb forms
            Most verbs have an open form (the dictionary form, used in habitual present) and a closed form (used after να, θα, αν).
            Ξυπνάω κάθε μέρα — open, habitual
            Θέλω να ξυπνήσω — closed after να
            Θα σου δείξω — closed after θα (from δείχνω)
            πότε vs. ποτέ — accent changes the meaning
            πότε (accent on the first syllable) = when
            ποτέ (accent on the last syllable) = never
            Demonstratives need the article in between
            In Greek, "this/that + noun" is this + the + noun. The article sits between the demonstrative and the noun, and both agree with the noun's gender, number, and case.
            ✅ αυτό το βιβλίο — this book  ·  ❌ αυτό βιβλίο
            ✅ αυτή την εβδομάδα — this week  ·  ❌ αυτή εβδομάδα
            ✅ αυτός ο άντρας — this man  ·  ❌ αυτός άντρας
            πολύ vs. πολλή/πολύς — adverb or adjective?
            πολύ (invariable adverb) modifies verbs and adjectives — it never changes form.
            πολύς / πολλή / πολύ / πολλοί / πολλές / πολλά (adjective) modifies nouns — agrees with gender and number.
            Είμαι πολύ κουρασμένος — adverb modifying adjective
            Δουλεύω πολύ — adverb modifying verb
            Πολλή δουλειά — adjective modifying fem. noun
            Πολλά παιδιά — adjective modifying neut. plural noun
            Test: what does it modify? Verb or adjective → πολύ. Noun → adjective form that agrees.
            αυτός vs. εκείνος — near vs. far
            αυτός / αυτή / αυτό = this (near, or default when distance doesn't matter).
            εκείνος / εκείνη / εκείνο = that (far, or contrasting "that one over there, not this one").
            Αυτό το βιβλίο είναι καλό — This book is good.
            Εκείνο το βιβλίο, όχι αυτό — That book, not this one.
            In casual spoken Greek, αυτός covers both. Reach for εκείνος only when you're explicitly contrasting near vs. far.
            🎭 Aspect-trigger cheat sheet — which form to pick
            Greek splits past, future, and subjunctive by aspect. Lock the trigger words and the choice is automatic.
            TriggerAspectExample
            πάντα · συνήθως · κάθε · τώραImperfectiveΠάντα μιλάω ελληνικά
            αύριο · απόψε · σε λίγο (with θα)PerfectiveΑύριο θα μιλήσω
            όταν · αν · πριν (future event)PerfectiveΌταν τελειώσω, θα φύγω
            να + θέλω / μπορώ / πρέπει (one-time act)PerfectiveΘέλω να μάθω αυτή τη λέξη
            να + θέλω / μπορώ (habitual / ongoing)ImperfectiveΜου αρέσει να διαβάζω
            The reflex: spot the trigger word first, then choose the aspect. If you can't find a trigger, the question is which mental picture you want — repeated/ongoing (imperfective) or single event (perfective).
            🔄 Perfective stem-change patterns
            Most regular verbs follow one of these letter swaps when forming the perfective. Once you know the ending of the present, the perfective is predictable.
            Present ends inPerfectiveExamples
            vowel + -ω · -ζω-σωαγοράζω → αγοράσω · διαβάζω → διαβάσω · ακούω → ακούσω
            -πω · -βω · -φω · -ευω · -αυω · -πτω-ψωγράφω → γράψω · δουλεύω → δουλέψω · ταξιδεύω → ταξιδέψω
            -κω · -γω · -χω-ξωτρέχω → τρέξω · ανοίγω → ανοίξω · προσέχω → προσέξω
            -χνω (drops the ν)-ξωφτιάχνω → φτιάξω · ψάχνω → ψάξω
            -τω · -δω · -θω · -νω-σωκαθαρίζω → καθαρίσω · χάνω → χάσω · πιστεύω → πιστέψω
            B-class -άω / -ώ-ήσωμιλάω → μιλήσω · ξυπνάω → ξυπνήσω · περπατάω → περπατήσω
            Trap to lock in: φτιάχνω → φτιάξω (not φτιαχσω). The χν combination collapses to ξ — there's no σ in there.
            The other trap: irregular dual-stem verbs (βλέπω/δω, λέω/πω, βρίσκω/βρω, βάζω/βάλω, παίρνω/πάρω, δίνω/δώσω, μαθαίνω/μάθω, τρώω/φάω, πίνω/πιω, φεύγω/φύγω, βγαίνω/βγω, έρχομαι/έρθω, πάω/πάω) don't follow these rules. Memorize as fixed chunks — see the table in Foundations → Verbs → Part 6.
            🧩 Cases — the article changes by role
            The article shifts depending on the noun's role in the sentence.
            CaseMasc.Fem.Neut.Used when…
            Nominativeοητοdescribing the subject
            Genitiveτουτηςτουpossession ("of the…")
            Accusativeτοντηντοdirect object, after prepositions
            Masculine drops the -ς in accusative: ο πατέρας (nom.) → τον πατέρα (acc.). Same for ο φίλος → τον φίλο.
            Prepositions like σε contract with the article: σε + τον = στον, σε + την = στην, σε + το = στο.
            🔤 Gender Endings — spot it from the ending
            Masculine: -ος, -ας, -ης
            ο άνθρωπος (o ánthropos) — the person
            ο πατέρας (o patéras) — the father
            ο μαθητής (o mathitís) — the student
            Feminine: -α, -η (and rarely -ος)
            η μητέρα (i mitéra) — the mother
            η αδελφή (i adelfí) — the sister
            η οδός (i odós) — the street — exception, looks masculine but is feminine
            Neuter: -ο, -ι, -μα
            το βιβλίο (to vivlío) — the book
            το παιδί (to pedí) — the child
            το πρόβλημα (to próvlima) — the problem
            -μα words are always neuter: πράγμα (thing), πρόβλημα (problem), θέμα (topic), μάθημα (lesson)…
            ⚡ Quick rule of thumb
            Ends in -ς → probably masculine
            Ends in -α or -η → probably feminine
            Ends in -ο, -ι, or -μα → probably neuter
            All feminine words end in η as their article, even when the noun itself ends in -α (like η μητέρα) or -ος (like η οδός).
            📦 Indefinite Articles (a / an) — Full Table
            The indefinite article changes by case and gender. Same rule as the definite article, different forms.
            CaseMasc. sing.Fem. sing.Neut. sing.
            Nominative
            (subject)            		 ένας (enas) μια / μία (mia) ένα (ena)
            Accusative
            (object, after most prepositions)            		 έναν (enan) μια / μία (mia) ένα (ena)
            Genitive
            (possession, "of a")            		 ενός (enos) μιας / μίας (mias) ενός (enos)
            Vocative
            (calling someone)            		 ———
            No plurals. Greek has no plural indefinite article. "Some friends" / "friends" → drop the article entirely, or use μερικοί / μερικές / μερικά for "some / a few." That's why the table is singular-only.
            🔍 Quick Reference: Gender Recognition by Noun Ending
            Spot the gender from the ending → pick the article without thinking.
            Noun endingUsual genderArticle (nom.)Examples
            -οςMasculineέναςένας φίλος, ένας καιρός, ένας άνθρωπος
            -αςMasculineέναςένας άντρας, ένας πατέρας, ένας μήνας
            -ηςMasculineέναςένας μαθητής, ένας πελάτης
            -αFeminineμιαμια μέρα, μια ώρα, μια δουλειά
            -ηFeminineμιαμια φίλη, μια αδερφή, μια τέχνη
            -ση / -ξη / -ψηFeminineμιαμια απόφαση, μια λέξη
            -ιNeuterέναένα παιδί, ένα τσάι, ένα τραγούδι
            -μαNeuterέναένα μάθημα, ένα πρόβλημα, ένα γράμμα
            -ος ⚠️Sometimes Neuterέναένα λάθος, ένα μέρος, ένα δάσος
            The -ος trap: most -ος words are masculine (ένας φίλος), but a small set is neuter (ένα λάθος = a mistake, ένα μέρος = a place, ένα δάσος = a forest). And the famous outlier η οδός (the street) is feminine. When in doubt, learn the noun with its article.
            🎯 Macro Patterns — Fix 80% of Errors

            From the 11/05 writing-practice feedback. Internalize these seven and dozens of future mistakes vanish.

            1. Every "ee" sound is a spelling decision
            Greek has 5+ ways to write "ee": η, ι, υ, ει, οι. Each word has a fixed spelling — there's no rule, just memory. Pause every time you write an "ee" sound; don't auto-default to ι.
            είναι (ει) · σήμερα (η) · δύσκολη (υ) · εκεί (ει) · νωρίς (ι)
            2. Verb endings are non-negotiable
            Present tense:
            1st person = -ω (Έχω, not Έχο)
            2nd person = -εις (έχεις)
            3rd person = -ει (έχει)
            Mixing 2nd and 3rd person changes WHO is doing the action. κάνεις = "you do." κάνει = "he/she does." Same root, different subject.
            3. Gender agreement is a chain
            Article + adjective + noun must all match in gender and number. If you know one piece, the others follow.
            GenderArticleAdjectiveNoun
            Feminineη / μίανέαφίλη
            Masculineο / έναςνέοςφίλος
            Neuterτο / ένανέοπαιδί
            Gender carries across sentences too. If you said ο καιρός last sentence, "it's bad" continues with masc.: Είναι κακός — not κακό.
            4. δ and θ are different sounds
            δ = soft "th" as in this.
            θ = hard "th" as in thin.
            "Don't / not" is δεν — soft th. Writing θεν says a nonsense word. Same trap: δουλειά (work), not θουλειά.
            5. σ becomes ς at the end of every word
            Middle of a word: σ.
            End of a word: ς.
            No exceptions. καιρός, φίλος, μαθητής, νωρίς.
            6. Greek has no present continuous
            English "is + verb-ing" → Greek simple present. There is no είναι + verb construction.
            "She is traveling" → ταξιδεύει (not είναι ταξιδεύει).
            "I am eating" → τρώω. "He is sleeping" → κοιμάται.
            This is the single most common English-thinking error. Drop "is" whenever you're tempted to add it before a verb.
            🥤 Worked example: πίνω (to drink)
            The "is it πίνω or πιω?" trap is just rule #2 (verb endings) + the aspect distinction. Same root, two forms based on what kind of action you mean.
            PersonHabitual (open)One-time after να/θα (closed / perfective)
            Iπίνωνα πιω
            You (sg.)πίνειςνα πιεις
            He / sheπίνεινα πιει
            Weπίνουμε—
            You allπίνετε—
            Theyπίνουν—
            Aspect cue: "every day" / "always" / "never" → habitual → use πίνω.
            "now" / "today" / "I want to…" / after να or θα → one-time → use πιω.
            ✓ Πίνω καφέ κάθε μέρα. — habitual
            ✓ Θέλω να πιω καφέ τώρα. — one-time
            Same pattern applies to most type-A verbs: ξυπνάω ↔ ξυπνήσω, διαβάζω ↔ διαβάσω, βλέπω ↔ δω.
            7. Articles where English doesn't have them
            Greek demands the definite article in three places where English drops it:
            ContextEnglishGreek
            Possessives"my homework"η εργασία μου
            Proper nouns after prepositions"with Niko"με τον Νίκο
            Most time expressions"after lesson"μετά το μάθημα
            When in doubt, add the article. Greek leans article-heavy.
            📋 May 14 Rule Codebook

            Severity-coded rules from the May 14 trainer compilation. 🔴 critical = causes communication failure. 🟠 high = clearly non-native. 🟡 marker = worth fixing eventually.

            R1🔴 criticalDouble negation table — every negation word needs δεν
            Greek negates with δεν before the verb + the negation word. Without δεν, the negation word flips to positive meaning.
            EnglishGreek (with δεν)Drops δεν? Means…
            neverδεν…ποτέ"ever" (positive)
            nothingδεν…τίποτα"anything"
            no one (m / f)δεν…κανείς / καμία"anyone / someone"
            nowhereδεν…πουθενά(unusable)
            The reflex to drill: when English has never / nothing / no one / nowhere, the FIRST word out of your mouth must be Δεν.
            R2🔴 criticalείναι is required — no copula drop
            Unlike Russian / Arabic, Greek requires the "is / are" verb. Subject + adjective alone is broken.
            ❌ Η φίλη μου κουρασμένη
            ✅ Η φίλη μου είναι κουρασμένη
            Same for "the weather is bad" → Ο καιρός είναι κακός.
            R10🟠 highκάθε ≠ any / some
            EnglishGreekNotes
            every / eachκάθεInvariable, positive frequency only
            some (m / f / n)κάποιος / κάποια / κάποιοGender-matches the noun
            any (in negation)— (drop it) OR καμία / κανένας"I don't want any" → just Δεν θέλω τίποτα
            δεν + κάθε is a near-contradiction. If English says "any / some" in a negative, drop the word entirely.
            R11🟠 highMasculine -ος / -ας / -ης drops -ς in accusative
            After prepositions like με, σε, από, masculine nouns lose their final -ς. The article also changes (ο → τον).
            NominativeAccusative
            ο φίλοςμε τον φίλο
            ο πατέραςμε τον πατέρα
            ο Νίκοςμε τον Νίκο
            ο Ιούλιοςτον Ιούλιο
            R14🟡 markerευ diphthong = "ef / ev"
            ευ before unvoiced consonants → "ef." Before vowels / voiced → "ev."
            Δευτέρα (def-) · ευχαριστώ (ef-) · Ευρώπη (ev-)
            R15🟡 markerαυ diphthong = "af / av"
            Same pattern as ευ. αυ before unvoiced → "af." Before voiced → "av."
            αύριο (av-) · αυτό (af-) · Αύγουστος (av-)
            R16🟡 markerου digraph = "oo"
            Two letters, one "oo" sound.
            δουλειά · Ιούλιος · ευχαριστούμε
            R17🟡 markerσ vs. ς vs. ζ — three different things
            σ and ς are the same letter in different positions — never end with σ.
            ζ is zeta — a totally different letter, used for the "z" sound (ζώο = animal).
            R18🟡 markerGreek rarely doubles consonants
            When in doubt, single. Watch θέλει (single λ, not θέλλει).
            Exceptions to memorize: άλλος, Ελλάδα, θάλασσα, Στέλλα, εννέα, γράμμα, Σάββατο.
            R19🟡 markerπριν vs. προτού
            πριν / πριν από + noun: πριν από το γραφείο = before the office.
            προτού + verb / clause: προτού φύγει = before he leaves.
            Never προτού before a noun.
            R20🟡 markerAdverbs never take articles
            σήμερα, αύριο, νωρίς, εδώ stand alone — no η / ο / το.
            ❌ η σήμερα · ✅ σήμερα
            🇬🇷 Greece Trip Prep — Tier 1 + Tier 2

            The 11 highest-leverage skills for pre-Greece prep, in priority order. Tier 1 is shipping-critical; Tier 2 is "first weeks in country." Tier 3 (genitive, conditional, perfect tense, aspect) intentionally deferred.

            T1.1🔴 unlockAorist (Past Tense) — single highest-impact skill
            Unlocks "what did you do today?" — ~40% of conversation. Regular pattern:
            PresentAorist (I-form)Pattern
            ταξιδεύωταξίδεψα-εύω → -εψα
            διαβάζωδιάβασα-ζω → -σα
            μιλάωμίλησα-άω → -ησα
            κάνωέκαναε- augment
            Critical irregulars (memorize as shapes):
            PresentAoristEnglish
            πάωπήγαI went
            βλέπωείδαI saw
            τρώωέφαγαI ate
            πίνωήπιαI drank
            έχωείχαI had
            είμαιήμουνI was
            λέωείπαI said
            βρίσκωβρήκαI found
            παίρνωπήραI took
            έρχομαιήρθαI came
            Practice prompt: "What did you do yesterday?" Try answering with 3-5 aorist verbs. Drill the ⏪ Past Tense chip and the new Past Tense Grammar Drill mode.
            T1.2🟠 highFuture = θα + perfective verb
            Simplest tense to learn. Future is just θα + the perfective (closed) form of the verb.
            EnglishGreek
            I will goθα πάω
            I will seeθα δω
            I will eatθα φάω
            I will be thereθα είμαι εκεί
            We will travelθα ταξιδέψουμε
            Travel/logistics use: booking, confirming, planning. "I will arrive on Monday." "I'll call you." Critical for surviving Greece independently.
            T1.3🔴 criticalQuestion words + intonation
            GreekEnglishNote
            Πού;Where?Location
            Πότε;When?Accent on first — vs. ποτέ (never)
            Τι;What?Universal
            Ποιος / Ποια / Ποιο;Who? / Which?Matches gender of the noun
            Γιατί;Why?Also means "because" mid-sentence
            Πόσο; / Πόσα;How much? / How many?Amounts, prices, durations
            Πώς;How?ω + accent. Don't confuse with πως (that).
            Question formation is INTONATION only — no auxiliary shuffle like English.
            Έχεις ψωμί. (You have bread.) → Έχεις ψωμί; (Do you have bread?) — same words, rising tone.
            T1.4🟠 highObject pronouns — same words, different positions
            μου, σου, του, της, μας, σας, τους serve TWO roles:
            RolePositionExample
            Possessive (after noun)Afterο φίλος μου (my friend)
            Indirect object (to me / to you)BEFORE the verbΜου είπε (he told me)
            Direct object pronouns (the / it / him / her / them):
            Gender / numberAcc. pronounExample
            Masc. sing.τονΤον είδα (I saw him)
            Fem. sing.τηνΤην ξέρω (I know her)
            Neut. sing.τοΤο ξέρω (I know it)
            Plural (m/f/n)τους / τις / ταΤους ξέρω (I know them)
            Word order: pronoun BEFORE the verb. Greek drops nouns once context is established and uses these pronouns.
            T1.5🟠 high"I like" = backwards: Μου αρέσει
            Greek doesn't say "I like X." It says "X is pleasing to me."
            EnglishGreekVerb form
            I like coffeeΜου αρέσει ο καφές.singular (one thing)
            I like fish (plural)Μου αρέσουν τα ψάρια.plural (multiple things)
            Do you like it?Σου αρέσει;
            She likes GreeceΤης αρέσει η Ελλάδα.
            The verb agrees with the THING LIKED, not with "me." One thing → αρέσει, many things → αρέσουν. Used dozens of times daily in restaurants, shopping, small talk.
            T1.6🟠 highNumbers 1–100 — cold-recall for every transaction
            1–20 are individual words. 21+ are tens + ones:
            NumberGreek
            10δέκα
            20είκοσι
            25είκοσι πέντε
            50πενήντα
            100εκατό
            1000χίλια
            Prices: "€8.50" = οκτώ και πενήντα ("eight and fifty"). Integer + και + cents.
            Gender of "one": ένας (m) / μία (f) / ένα (n). For "two" and up, no gender distinction except 3 and 4 (τρία/τρεις, τέσσερα/τέσσερις).
            T1.7🟡 markerImperatives — comprehension first, production second
            You'll hear these constantly. You don't need to produce them all.
            GreekEnglishContext
            Πάμε!Let's go!Universal "shall we?"
            Έλα! / Ελάτε!Come! (sg / pl)Server, host, parent
            Πες μου / Δώσε μουTell me / Give meImperative + pronoun
            Περίμενε / ΣταμάταWait / StopCommon directions
            Κοίτα / ΆκουLook / ListenConversational lead-ins
            Καλώς ήρθες / ήρθατεWelcome! (sg / pl)Standard hospitality
            Negative imperative uses μη + present tense, NOT δεν. "Don't talk!" = Μη μιλάς!
            T2.8🟠 highFormal vs Informal "you"
            Same verb, different endings based on relationship:
            VerbInformal (εσύ)Formal (εσείς)
            How are you?Τι κάνεις;Τι κάνετε;
            What's your name?Πώς σε λένε;Πώς σας λένε;
            Do you want…?Θέλεις...Θέλετε...
            Do you have…?Έχεις...Έχετε...
            Default to formal with anyone over ~30 you don't know. Shopkeepers, waiters, older strangers all want εσείς. Getting this wrong reads as rude. Informal is for friends, family, peers, kids.
            T2.9🟡 markerColloquialisms — what makes you not sound like a textbook
            This is where textbook Greek and real Greek diverge.
            GreekReal meaning
            Τι κάνεις;"How are you?" (not "what are you doing")
            Σιγά σιγάTake it easy / chill
            Δεν πειράζειNo worries / doesn't matter
            Έλα ντε!Right?! / Exactly!
            Δεν βαριέσαιWhatever / can't be bothered
            Όπα!Whoa! (surprise or playful warning)
            This is your father's comparative advantage. Ask him for the 20 most-used colloquialisms that aren't in textbooks. The pre-populated 💥 Colloquialisms chip is a starting placeholder — revise once you have his real list.
            T2.10🟠 highSubjunctive: να + perfective verb
            Unlocks "want to / need to / can / should" — a huge category.
            TriggerExampleEnglish
            θέλω ναΘέλω να πάω.I want to go.
            πρέπει ναΠρέπει να φύγω.I have to leave.
            μπορώ ναΜπορώ να σε βοηθήσω;Can I help you?
            The verb after να is the PERFECTIVE (closed) form, not the present. πάω → να πάω, φεύγω → να φύγω, βοηθάω → να βοηθήσω.
            T1.3b🔴 criticalQuestion Starters — the templates real questions begin with
            Single question words (πού / πότε / τι) are the building blocks. These are the assembled openers you use every day in Greece.
            PatternUseExample
            Πού είναι...;Locate somethingΠού είναι η τουαλέτα;
            Πόσο κάνει;Ask priceΠόσο κάνει ο καφές;
            Τι ώρα...;Time / opening hoursΤι ώρα ανοίγει;
            Μπορώ να...;Permission / requestΜπορώ να δω το μενού;
            Μπορείτε να...;Polite request (formal)Μπορείτε να με βοηθήσετε;
            Έχετε...;"Do you have..." (shops)Έχετε νερό;
            Πώς πάω στο...;DirectionsΠώς πάω στο μουσείο;
            Πώς λέγεται...;What's this called?Πώς λέγεται αυτό;
            Από πού...;OriginΑπό πού είσαι;
            Politeness shields — stick any of these in front of a question and it softens the whole thing:
            Συγγνώμη,...; = Excuse me,...?
            Με συγχωρείτε,...; = Excuse me,... (formal)
            Παρακαλώ,...; = Please,...?
            Μπορώ να σε ρωτήσω...; = Can I ask you...?
            Yes/no questions = intonation only. Same word order as a statement, just rising tone at the end:
            Έχεις χρόνο. (statement) → Έχεις χρόνο; (question)
            T2.11🟡 markerConnector words — speak in sentences, not phrases
            GreekEnglishUse
            καιandJoins clauses or items
            αλλάbutContrast
            όμωςhoweverSlightly more formal
            επειδή / γιατίbecauseMid-sentence γιατί = "because"
            ανifConditional
            ότανwhenTemporal subordinator (not the question)
            πρώτα ... μετά ...first ... then ...Sequencing
            λοιπόνso / well / anywayFiller / transition
            Without these your speech is choppy sentences with no flow. With them, you sound like you're thinking in Greek, not translating.
            🎯 Drill Priorities (May 18 takeaways)

            From analyzing the 4-task exercise corpus (~25/42 grammar correct). Use this to spend your daily drill time on what's actually leaking, not what's already locked.

            🚨 Persistent leaks — drill these first
            LeakSeverity
            Person mismatch — defaulting to 1st/3rd singular when subject demands 2nd plural (Εσείς → -ετε), 3rd plural (τα παιδιά → -ουν), etc.🔴 top
            Irregular dual-stem verbs — applying -ησω/-ψω rules to verbs that have totally different stems (φεύγω → φύγ-, not φευγσ-; βάζω → βαλ-; βρίσκω → βρ-)🔴 top
            Aspect after time adverbs (πάντα → imperfective vs αύριο+θα → perfective)🟠
            χν → ξ collapse (φτιάχνω → φτιάξω, NOT φτιαχσω)🟠
            Missing δεν before κανείς / τίποτα / ποτέ🟠
            Masculine accusative drop (πατέρας → πατέρα)🟡
            TE rule regression (στη(ν) overuse on days/months)🟡
            The metacognitive fix for person mismatch: before conjugating, name the subject's person/number out loud. "Εσείς is 2nd plural → -ετε." That single discipline step eliminates ~6 errors per session.
            Tonight's session: 📚 Verb Pairs (SRS) for irregular dual-stem verbs · 🎭 Aspect drill · 🗂️ Verb Group Classifier.
            🔒 Locked patterns — maintain weekly, don't burn daily time
            These won't regress with light maintenance. One pass per week is enough.
            • 1st-person -ω endings (Πίνω, Έχω, ξυπνάω)
            • 3rd-person -ει endings (έχει, πίνει, θέλει)
            • Possessive structure (ο/η + noun + μου)
            • με + τον/τη contractions
            • από + article + noun
            • Feminine indefinite chain (μια + adj-η + noun-η)
            • Neuter chain for -μα nouns (ένα + adj-ο + μάθημα)
            • ξυπνάω verb family
            • σ vs ς at word end
            • ευ diphthong (Δευτέρα)
            💡 Mistake reflections — rules learned the hard way
            1. δεν is a reflex, not a knowledge gap. The rule is in your head; what fails is firing it in real-time. Speed drills > rule explanations.
            2. Months are vocab, not grammar. No rule will save you if Ιούλιος isn't a memorized shape.
            3. English "in / on" + time → drop the preposition for days, months, times-of-day. Only clock times keep στις.
            4. κάθε = every, never any / some. δεν + κάθε is a near-contradiction.

            📓 My Personal Vocabulary

            Add words and phrases as you learn them. They save to your browser and stay here between visits.

            📅 Today's 5 new words

            loading…
            Loading today's picks…

            + Add a new entry

            ✓ Added!
            By type:
            Word / Phrase Translation Language Topic Edit

            📖 Foundations of Greek Grammar

            Beginner-level explainers. Start here if you've never built a Greek sentence from first principles — these walk through the WHY behind everything you've been drilling.

            Verbs carry more information inside the word in Greek than in English. The ending of the verb tells you WHO is doing the action — so the pronoun is optional. Learn one verb's six person-endings and you've cracked the system. Click any part below to expand.

            A verb is a word that names an action or a state of being. In English: I walk, you eat, she is, they sleep. Greek works the same way — every sentence has a verb at its core.

            But Greek verbs carry far more information inside the word itself than English verbs do. This is the single most important thing to internalize.

            In English, the verb "walk" tells you almost nothing on its own. You need a separate pronoun:

            I walk, you walk, we walk, they walk — same word "walk," four different meanings.

            In Greek, the verb itself tells you who's doing it. The ending changes:

            GreekEnglishWhat the ending means
            περπατάωI walk-ω = "I"
            περπατάςyou walk-ας = "you"
            περπατάειhe/she/it walks-αει = "he/she/it"
            περπατάμεwe walk-αμε = "we"
            περπατάτεyou all walk-ατε = "you all"
            περπατάνεthey walk-ανε = "they"

            You don't say εγώ περπατάω the way you say "I walk" in English. The εγώ is optional and usually dropped, because the -ω ending already tells you "I."

            The first mental shift: in Greek, the ending of the verb IS the subject pronoun. You're not learning a verb — you're learning six forms of every verb.

            Every verb in every language can be conjugated for six grammatical "persons." Memorize these labels — every Greek grammar book uses them.

            PersonGreek pronounEnglishExample
            1st singularεγώII walk
            2nd singularεσύyou (one person)you walk
            3rd singularαυτός / αυτή / αυτόhe / she / ithe walks
            1st pluralεμείςwewe walk
            2nd pluralεσείςyou all (or formal "you")you all walk
            3rd pluralαυτοί / αυτές / αυτάtheythey walk
            Important: 2nd plural (εσείς) is also the formal "you" for a single person — like French vous or Spanish usted. Use it with strangers, shopkeepers, older people.

            When you look up a verb in a Greek dictionary, you find the 1st person singular present form. So the dictionary entry is "I ___," not "to ___."

            "To walk" in a Greek dictionary appears as περπατάω, literally "I walk."

            Greek has no infinitive ("to walk") at all in modern usage — it was lost over the centuries. We'll see what replaced it in Part 8.

            Greek verbs fall into a few conjugation patterns. Which pattern depends on where the stress lands and what the 1st singular looks like.

            Family A — stress on the stem, unstressed -ω ending
            Example: γράφω (I write). GRAH-fo — stress on stem.

            FormPhoneticMeaning
            γράφωGRAH-foI write
            γράφειςGRAH-feesyou write
            γράφειGRAH-feehe/she writes
            γράφουμεGRAH-foo-mewe write
            γράφετεGRAH-fe-teyou all write
            γράφουνGRAH-foonthey write

            Endings: -ω, -εις, -ει, -ουμε, -ετε, -ουν. Most Greek verbs are Family A: βλέπω, τρώω, πίνω, μένω, παίρνω, δίνω, έχω, κάνω, ακούω, αγοράζω.

            Family B1 — stress on ending, "-άς" in 2nd person
            Example: αγαπάω (I love). ah-gha-PAH-oh.

            FormMeaning
            αγαπάωI love
            αγαπάςyou love
            αγαπάειhe/she loves
            αγαπάμεwe love
            αγαπάτεyou all love
            αγαπάνεthey love

            Endings: -άω, -άς, -άει, -άμε, -άτε, -άνε. From your vocab: ξυπνάω, περπατάω, μιλάω, ρωτάω.

            Family B2 — stress on ending, "-είς" in 2nd person
            Example: οδηγώ (I drive). oh-thee-GHOH.

            FormMeaning
            οδηγώI drive
            οδηγείςyou drive
            οδηγείhe/she drives
            οδηγούμεwe drive
            οδηγείτεyou all drive
            οδηγούνthey drive

            Endings: -ώ, -είς, -εί, -ούμε, -είτε, -ούν. From your vocab: μπορώ, βοηθώ, ευχαριστώ, παρακαλώ.

            How to tell B1 from B2: You can't reliably tell from the 1st singular (both end in -ώ). You memorize the 2nd singular alongside the 1st. αγαπώ → αγαπάς = B1. οδηγώ → οδηγείς = B2.

            Family M — middle/passive, ends in -μαι
            Example: κοιμάμαι (I sleep). Conjugates completely differently (Part 7 explains why):

            κοιμάμαιI sleep
            κοιμάσαιyou sleep
            κοιμάταιhe/she sleeps
            κοιμόμαστεwe sleep
            κοιμάστεyou all sleep
            κοιμούνταιthey sleep

            This is the single most important new concept. Aspect = the difference between ongoing/habitual action and a single completed event.

            English has hints. Compare: "I was reading when she called" (ongoing) vs. "I read the book yesterday" (completed). "I eat fish" (habitual) vs. "I ate the fish" (one event).

            English makes these distinctions partially. Greek bakes the distinction into the verb stem itself. Every action verb has TWO completely separate stems:

            Imperfective form — for ongoing, habitual, repeated, or in-progress action. Use when: action is happening now, happens regularly, or has no defined endpoint. This is the form you've been learning: γράφω.

            Perfective form (aorist) — for a single completed event, viewed as a whole. Use when: one bounded action, future actions that happen once (with θα), past actions completed, after να. Different stem of the same verb: γράψω.

            Imperfective (process)Perfective (event)Meaning
            γράφωγράψωI write / I'll write
            διαβάζωδιαβάσωI read / I'll read
            τρώωφάωI eat / I'll eat
            πίνωπιωI drink / I'll drink
            βλέπωδωI see / I'll see
            παίρνωπάρωI take / I'll take
            μαθαίνωμάθωI learn / I'll learn
            λέωπωI say / I'll say
            πάωπάω (same)I go / I'll go

            Stem changes are sometimes predictable (φ → ψ, ζ → σ) and sometimes wildly irregular (τρώω → φάω).

            Critical: in isolation, γράψω doesn't mean anything specific — it's a stem waiting for context. You almost never use a perfective form alone. You use it after θα (will), να (to/that), or with the past prefix ε-.

            Tense = when the action happened. Six tenses in Greek; here are the four essentials.

            Present tense — imperfective stem + basic endings.

            • γράφω — I write / I'm writing
            • τρώω — I eat / I'm eating
            • περπατάω — I walk / I'm walking

            Future continuous — θα + imperfective stem. "I'll be doing it / I'll do it habitually."

            • θα γράφω — I'll be writing (ongoing)
            • θα τρώω — I'll be eating (regularly)

            Future simple — θα + perfective (aorist) stem. "I'll do it once." This is the everyday future.

            • θα γράψω — I'll write (one time)
            • θα φάω — I'll eat (one specific meal)
            • θα πάω — I'll go (one trip)

            Aorist past — ε- prefix + aorist stem + past endings (-α, -ες, -ε, -αμε, -ατε, -αν).

            Example with γράφω → aorist stem γράψ-:

            • έγραψα (I wrote)
            • έγραψες (you wrote)
            • έγραψε (he/she wrote)
            • γράψαμε (we wrote) — ε- disappears, stress moves
            • γράψατε (you all wrote)
            • έγραψαν (they wrote)

            Critical irregular aorists — drill these as fixed shapes:

            • πήγα — I went
            • είδα — I saw
            • έφαγα — I ate
            • ήπια — I drank
            • είπα — I said
            • πήρα — I took
            • έκανα — I did/made
            • ήμουν — I was
            • ήθελα — I wanted
            • βρήκα — I found

            The 12 high-frequency irregular verbs — all four forms

            Each verb has four essential forms. Memorize as a chunk per verb. The "to V" column is the bare perfective stem you put after θα, να, or αν; it's also the form you use to talk about a single completed event.

            Verb to V
            να + perfective            		 Present
            imperfective            		 Past
            aorist            		 Past continuous
            imperfect
            to seeνα δωβλέπωείδαέβλεπα
            to sayνα πωλέωείπαέλεγα
            to eatνα φάωτρώωέφαγαέτρωγα
            to goνα πάωπηγαίνωπήγαπήγαινα
            to drinkνα πιωπίνωήπιαέπινα
            to findνα βρωβρίσκωβρήκαέβρισκα
            to comeνα έρθωέρχομαιήρθαερχόμουν
            to takeνα πάρωπαίρνωπήραέπαιρνα
            to giveνα δώσωδίνωέδωσαέδινα
            to learnνα μάθωμαθαίνωέμαθαμάθαινα
            to leaveνα φύγωφεύγωέφυγαέφευγα
            to go outνα βγωβγαίνωβγήκαέβγαινα
            to put / wearνα βάλωβάζωέβαλαέβαζα
            Drill move: say all four forms in sequence for each verb — να δω · βλέπω · είδα · έβλεπα. That cadence is what locks them in. The aorist is the irregular one to memorize first; the imperfect is mostly predictable (ε- + present stem with shift).

            Voice = who is doing what to whom.

            Active voice — subject does the action to something else.
            γράφω ένα γράμμα — I write a letter.
            95% of your verbs are active.

            Passive voice — subject receives the action.
            το γράμμα γράφεται — the letter is being written.
            English uses "is being ___ed." Greek uses different endings (the -μαι family).

            Middle voice — the action happens to/for the subject themselves. This doesn't exist in English.
            κοιμάμαι (I sleep) — sleeping happens to/within me.
            ντύνομαι (I get dressed) — I dress myself.
            σκέφτομαι (I think) — thinking happens inside me.

            In Greek, the same -μαι endings are used for both middle and passive. Context disambiguates.

            Deponent verbs — look middle, mean active: εύχομαι (I wish), έρχομαι (I come), ασχολούμαι (I'm occupied with). Memorize them with their -μαι ending — don't try to derive them.

            Mood = the speaker's stance: stating a fact? Wishing? Commanding?

            Indicative mood — plain statements of fact. What you've been using. γράφω (I write), πήγαμε (we went), θα φάω (I'll eat).

            Subjunctive mood — possibility, wish, purpose, "to ___." The big new one.
            Greek lost its infinitive. The subjunctive replaces it. When English uses "to ___" or "that ___" or "should ___," Greek uses να + a verb form.

            • θέλω να φάω — I want to eat
            • πρέπει να πάμε — we must go
            • μπορώ να βοηθήσω — I can help
            • θέλω να μάθω ελληνικά — I want to learn Greek
            • για να καταλάβω — so that I understand
            You'll use this constantly. Every "I want to / need to / can / try to" in English maps to θέλω/πρέπει/μπορώ/προσπαθώ + να + verb.

            After να, you use either imperfective or perfective depending on aspect:

            • θέλω να γράφω (I want to be writing / write habitually) — imperfective
            • θέλω να γράψω (I want to write once, complete it) — perfective

            Imperative mood — commands. See the dedicated ❗ Imperatives section.

            είμαι (I am) is the most-used verb in any language and it's irregular. Memorize the full conjugation now.

            PersonFormMeaning
            1st singείμαιI am
            2nd singείσαιyou are
            3rd singείναιhe/she/it is
            1st pluralείμαστεwe are
            2nd pluralείστε / είσαστεyou all / formal you
            3rd pluralείναιthey are (same as 3rd sing!)

            Past tense ("was/were"):

            • ήμουν (I was)
            • ήσουν (you were)
            • ήταν (he/she/it was)
            • ήμασταν (we were)
            • ήσασταν (you all were)
            • ήταν (they were — same as 3rd sing)
            είμαι has no perfective form — "being" isn't a one-time event, it's always a state.

            Every Greek verb carries answers to five questions simultaneously:

            1. Who? (person/number)
            2. When? (tense — present? future? past?)
            3. Process or event? (aspect — imperfective or perfective stem?)
            4. Who's acting on whom? (voice — active, middle, passive)
            5. Speaker's attitude? (mood — stating, wishing, commanding)

            Example: Θέλω να φάμε μαζί αύριο. (I want us to eat together tomorrow.)

            • θέλω — 1st sing present indicative active of θέλω
            • να — subjunctive marker
            • φάμε — 1st plural aorist subjunctive of τρώω
            • μαζί — together
            • αύριο — tomorrow

            Two verbs, both fully decoded once you know the system.

            Absolute minimum to drill before adding anything else:

            1. The six person endings of Family A (-ω, -εις, -ει, -ουμε, -ετε, -ουν). Drill on five verbs until automatic.
            2. The full conjugation of είμαι present and past. Irregular and unavoidable.
            3. The aorist pairs. γράφω ↔ γράψω, τρώω ↔ φάω, πίνω ↔ πιω. Drill in both directions.
            4. The θα + aorist = future pattern. θα γράψω, θα φάω, θα πάω.
            5. The θέλω/μπορώ/πρέπει + να + aorist pattern. Unlocks every "I want to ___" sentence.

            Once these are automatic: add past tense (ε- prefix), imperatives, and the full middle voice. 4-6 week roadmap at 20 minutes/day.

            → Drill in the 📚 Verb Pairs (SRS) mode of Grammar Drill

            Nouns are the people, places, things, and ideas in a sentence. Every Greek noun has a gender (masc / fem / neut) — and the gender controls everything: which article it takes, what adjective form goes with it, how it changes shape across cases. Once you know the gender of a noun, the rest follows mechanically.

            A noun is a word for a person, place, thing, or idea. House, woman, idea, Athens.

            In English, nouns are simple. They change for plural (one cat → two cats) and that's it. Greek nouns carry FOUR pieces of information inside the word:

            1. Gender — masculine, feminine, or neuter (assigned, not biological)
            2. Number — singular or plural
            3. Case — nominative, accusative, genitive, vocative (the noun's role in the sentence)
            4. Article — almost every noun comes with one (the / a)

            So a single Greek noun like τον φίλο tells you: it's masculine, singular, accusative, and definite. The English equivalent "the friend" only tells you "definite."

            Key habit: when you learn a new Greek noun, always memorize it WITH its article. Not σπίτι — learn το σπίτι. The article tells you the gender, and the gender tells you everything else.

            Every Greek noun is masculine, feminine, or neuter. Gender is grammatical, not biological — το κορίτσι (the girl) is neuter, η οδός (the street) is feminine.

            Spot the gender from the noun's ending:

            EndingUsual genderArticleExamples
            -οςMasculineοο φίλος, ο καιρός, ο άνθρωπος
            -αςMasculineοο πατέρας, ο άντρας, ο μήνας
            -ηςMasculineοο μαθητής, ο πελάτης
            -αFeminineηη μέρα, η ώρα, η μητέρα
            -ηFeminineηη φίλη, η αδερφή, η τέχνη
            -ση / -ξη / -ψηFeminineηη απόφαση, η λέξη
            -οNeuterτοτο βιβλίο, το σπίτι (no), wait — -ι is neuter
            -ιNeuterτοτο παιδί, το τσάι, το σπίτι
            -μαNeuterτοτο μάθημα, το πρόβλημα, το όνομα
            -ος ⚠️Sometimes neuterτοτο λάθος, το μέρος, το δάσος
            The trap: most -ος words are masculine, but a small set is neuter (λάθος = mistake, μέρος = place, δάσος = forest). And the famous outlier η οδός (the street) is feminine despite the -ος ending. Always learn the article with the noun.

            -μα is the most reliable ending in Greek: if a noun ends in -μα, it's ALWAYS neuter. Lock this rule and a whole class of gender errors disappears.

            Greek uses articles much more than English. Possessives, proper nouns after prepositions, time expressions, abstract concepts — all need articles in Greek that English drops.

            Definite article (the):

            CaseMasc.Fem.Neut.Pl. m / f / n
            Nominativeοητοοι / οι / τα
            Accusativeτοντηντοτους / τις / τα
            Genitiveτουτηςτουτων / των / των

            Indefinite article (a / an):

            CaseMasc.Fem.Neut.
            Nominativeέναςμία / μιαένα
            Accusativeένανμία / μιαένα
            Genitiveενόςμιαςενός
            No plural indefinite article. "Some friends" / "friends" → drop the article entirely, or use μερικοί / μερικές / μερικά (some / a few).

            Case = the form a noun takes based on what job it's doing. English uses word order for this; Greek changes the noun's ending.

            CaseWhat it doesExample role
            NominativeThe subject — who/what is doing the action"The cat sleeps."
            AccusativeThe direct object, or after most prepositions"I see the cat." / "with the cat"
            GenitivePossession ("of the…"), or some preps"the cat's tail" = "the tail of the cat"
            VocativeCalling/addressing someone"Hey, friend!"

            How nouns change endings: mostly the article changes, but masculine nouns drop their final -ς in the accusative.

            GenderNom.Acc.Gen.
            Masc. (-ος)ο φίλοςτον φίλο (drops -ς)του φίλου
            Masc. (-ας)ο πατέραςτον πατέρα (drops -ς)του πατέρα
            Fem. (-α)η μέρατη(ν) μέρα (same)της μέρας
            Fem. (-η)η φίλητη(ν) φίλη (same)της φίλης
            Neut. (-ο)το βιβλίοτο βιβλίο (same)του βιβλίου
            Neut. (-ι)το παιδίτο παιδί (same)του παιδιού
            The big rule: Masculine drops the final -ς in accusative. Feminine and neuter don't change the noun — only the article shifts.

            For most of your first conversations you only need nominative and accusative. Genitive comes later. Vocative is mostly for calling names: Γιώργο! (Hey George!).

            The preposition σε (to / at / in) always contracts with the following article. You'll see these constantly.

            σε + articleResultExample
            σε + τονστονστον φίλο (to the friend)
            σε + τηνστηνστην Αθήνα (to Athens)
            σε + τοστοστο σπίτι (at home)
            σε + τουςστουςστους φίλους
            σε + τιςστιςστις οκτώ (at 8)
            σε + τασταστα βόρεια
            Never write σε ο, σε τη, etc. The contraction is mandatory. Also: στιν is NOT a valid form (it's a mashup of στη + την that some learners produce).

            To say "my X" / "your X" / "his X," Greek uses two structures depending on emphasis:

            (1) The everyday way — possessive pronoun AFTER the noun:

            EnglishGreek
            my friendο φίλος μου
            your friendο φίλος σου
            his friendο φίλος του
            her friendο φίλος της
            our friendο φίλος μας
            your (pl) friendο φίλος σας
            their friendο φίλος τους

            The noun still keeps its article. Greek literally says "the friend my."

            (2) Emphatic — δικός μου ("of mine") agreeing in gender: ο δικός μου φίλος = "MY friend (not yours)."

            Critical: the same pronoun μου/σου/του/της/μας/σας/τους doubles as the indirect object pronoun when placed BEFORE a verb: Μου είπε = "He told me." Position changes the meaning.

            Greek pluralizes by changing the ending AND the article.

            GenderSing. nom.Plural nom.Sing. acc.Plural acc.
            Masc. (-ος)ο φίλοςοι φίλοιτον φίλοτους φίλους
            Masc. (-ας)ο πατέραςοι πατέρεςτον πατέρατους πατέρες
            Fem. (-α)η μέραοι μέρεςτη μέρατις μέρες
            Fem. (-η)η φίληοι φίλεςτη φίλητις φίλες
            Neut. (-ο)το βιβλίοτα βιβλίατο βιβλίοτα βιβλία
            Neut. (-ι)το παιδίτα παιδιάτο παιδίτα παιδιά
            Neut. (-μα)το μάθηματα μαθήματατο μάθηματα μαθήματα

            Plural is easier than singular: feminine and neuter plural have IDENTICAL nominative and accusative forms.

            1. Learn every new noun with its article. Not σπίτι, learn το σπίτι. Not φίλος, learn ο φίλος. The article tells you the gender — everything else follows.
            2. Memorize the gender-by-ending table. 80% of nouns follow it. The exceptions (η οδός, το λάθος) you learn as oddballs.
            3. -μα is ALWAYS neuter. One ironclad rule.
            4. Masculine drops -ς in accusative. ο φίλος → τον φίλο, ο πατέρας → τον πατέρα. Drill it.
            5. σε + article contractions. Never write σε + article uncontracted: στο / στη(ν) / στον / στους / στις / στα.
            6. Possessive pronoun comes AFTER the noun. Same word doubles as indirect object BEFORE a verb. Position = meaning.

            → Drill in the 📦 Noun Pairs (SRS) mode of Grammar Drill

            Adjectives describe nouns (big, red, tired, beautiful). In Greek, adjectives change form to match the noun in gender, number, and case. Once you can identify a noun's gender, the adjective ending follows mechanically. The adjective is the GLUE that binds the article and noun together — get them to agree and your Greek immediately stops sounding broken.

            In English, "big" is "big" no matter what noun follows: a big house, a big car, a big dog, big houses. The adjective never changes.

            In Greek, every adjective has at least THREE forms — one for each gender — and changes for plural and case too:

            NounGreekAdj. ending
            A big friend (m)ένας μεγάλος φίλος-ος (masc)
            A big day (f)μια μεγάλη μέρα-η (fem)
            A big problem (n)ένα μεγάλο πρόβλημα-ο (neut)

            Same word, three endings. The chain is article + adjective + noun, and all three must agree.

            The rule of three: if you know the noun's gender, the adjective ending follows automatically. Mismatch and your speech reads as broken Greek even if everything else is right.

            Most Greek adjectives follow this pattern:

            GenderSingularPlural
            Masculineκαλόςκαλοί
            Feminineκαλήκαλές
            Neuterκαλόκαλά

            Examples — all decline this same way:

            • μεγάλος / μεγάλη / μεγάλο — big
            • μικρός / μικρή / μικρό — small
            • νέος / νέα / νέο — new (irregular: fem ends in -α)
            • κουρασμένος / κουρασμένη / κουρασμένο — tired
            • ωραίος / ωραία / ωραίο — nice / beautiful (fem in -α)
            • δύσκολος / δύσκολη / δύσκολο — difficult
            • πλούσιος / πλούσια / πλούσιο — rich (fem in -α)
            Pattern variant: some adjectives use feminine -α instead of -η (νέα, ωραία, πλούσια). Just memorize which is which when you learn the adjective.

            When you say "X is Y" — the adjective still agrees with X, even with the verb είναι in between. είναι is gender-invisible; the adjective looks back at the subject.

            • Ο καιρός είναι κακός. — The weather is bad. (καιρός is masc → κακός)
            • Η εργασία είναι δύσκολη. — The homework is difficult. (εργασία is fem → δύσκολη)
            • Το μάθημα είναι δύσκολο. — The lesson is difficult. (μάθημα is neut → δύσκολο)
            Gender carries across sentences too. If the conversation is about ο καιρός, and you say "It's bad," that "bad" must be masculine: Είναι κακός. NOT κακό (neuter default), even though English "it" is neuter.

            Default order: article + adjective + noun.

            • η ωραία μέρα — the beautiful day
            • ένα μικρό σπίτι — a small house
            • οι κουρασμένοι φίλοι — the tired friends

            Adjective AFTER the noun happens in two cases:

            1. Predicate position (with είναι): Η μέρα είναι ωραία.
            2. Emphasis or definite article repetition: η μέρα η ωραία = "the day — the beautiful one." Rare in normal speech.

            Most Greek adverbs are formed from adjectives by changing the ending to -α (or using the neuter plural form). The adjective καλός (good) becomes the adverb καλά (well).

            Adjective (m / f / n)AdverbMeaning
            καλός / καλή / καλόκαλάgood → well
            γρήγορος / -η / -ογρήγοραfast → quickly
            αργός / -ή / -όαργάslow → slowly
            ωραίος / -α / -οωραίαnice → nicely
            εύκολος / -η / -οεύκολαeasy → easily

            This is why the same word καλά appears in "Τι κάνεις;" → "Καλά, εσύ;" — it's literally "Well, you?" using the adverb form.

            A few high-frequency adjectives don't follow the -ος/-η/-ο pattern. Memorize them:

            MeaningMasc.Fem.Neut.
            much / many / veryπολύςπολλήπολύ
            tall / high / longμακρύςμακριάμακρύ
            young / newνέοςνέανέο
            interestingενδιαφέρωνενδιαφέρουσαενδιαφέρον
            πολύ is the trap: πολύς agrees with the masc noun, πολλή (double λ!) with fem, πολύ with neuter AND as a universal "very" intensifier. Most beginners just use πολύ as "very" without worrying about the agreement.

            Comparative ("more X / X-er") — use πιο + adjective:

            • πιο μεγάλος — bigger
            • πιο γρήγορος — faster
            • πιο ωραίος — nicer

            "Than" is από + accusative or παρά:
            Είναι πιο μεγάλος από εμένα. — He's bigger than me.

            Superlative ("the most X / X-est") — use article + πιο + adjective:

            • ο πιο μεγάλος — the biggest
            • το πιο ωραίο σπίτι — the nicest house
            There's also a single-word comparative (μεγαλύτερος = bigger) but the πιο + adjective form works for any adjective and is what beginners use 95% of the time.
            1. The -ος / -η / -ο pattern. If you know one, you know dozens. Drill the chain: καλός / καλή / καλό.
            2. Adjective ending matches the noun. Article + adjective + noun all in the same gender. Mismatch = broken Greek.
            3. είναι is gender-invisible. Ο καιρός είναι κακός, NOT κακό. The adjective looks back to the subject.
            4. Adjective comes BEFORE the noun. After means predicate ("X is Y").
            5. πολύ as universal intensifier. Don't worry about agreement when using it adverbially.
            6. Comparative = πιο + adjective. Single rule unlocks all comparisons.

            → Drill in the 🎨 Adj Forms (SRS) mode of Grammar Drill

            Adverbs are the easiest part of Greek for English speakers. They don't change form — no gender, no number, no case. Memorize the word once and you're done. They modify verbs, adjectives, or whole sentences.

            An adverb modifies a verb, an adjective, or another adverb. In English: slowly, often, very, today, here.

            Adverbs answer questions like:

            • How? — καλά (well), γρήγορα (quickly), σιγά (slowly)
            • When? — σήμερα (today), αύριο (tomorrow), τώρα (now)
            • Where? — εδώ (here), εκεί (there)
            • How often? — πάντα (always), συχνά (often), ποτέ (never)
            • How much? — πολύ (very), λίγο (a little)
            The good news: Greek adverbs are INVARIABLE. They never agree with anything. Once you know γρήγορα, that's it — no plural form, no feminine form, no case changes.

            Most "manner" adverbs come from adjectives by changing the ending to -α (or -ά if the stress is at the end). It's the same as English adding "-ly":

            AdjectiveAdverbEnglish
            καλός (good)καλάwell
            γρήγορος (fast)γρήγοραquickly
            αργός (slow)αργάslowly
            ωραίος (nice)ωραίαnicely
            εύκολος (easy)εύκολαeasily
            δύσκολος (hard)δύσκολαwith difficulty
            ζεστός (warm)ζεστάwarmly
            όμορφος (beautiful)όμορφαbeautifully
            Trap: the adverb form (καλά) is identical to the neuter plural adjective form. Context tells you which: "Τα παιδιά είναι καλά" = "The kids are good (well)" — could be either reading.

            Time

            • σήμερα — today
            • αύριο — tomorrow
            • χθες — yesterday
            • τώρα — now
            • μετά — after
            • πριν — before
            • νωρίς — early
            • αργότερα — later
            • απόψε — tonight

            Place

            • εδώ — here
            • εκεί — there
            • πάνω — up / on top
            • κάτω — down / below
            • μέσα — inside
            • έξω — outside
            • κοντά — near
            • μακριά — far

            Frequency

            • πάντα — always
            • συνήθως — usually
            • συχνά — often
            • μερικές φορές — sometimes
            • σπάνια — rarely
            • ποτέ — never (with δεν!)

            Quantity / intensity

            • πολύ — very / a lot
            • πάρα πολύ — very much
            • λίγο — a little
            • σχεδόν — almost
            • μόνο — only
            • περίπου — about / approximately

            This is a top-3 beginner trap. English doesn't use articles with adverbs ("today," not "the today") — same in Greek. But because Greek loves articles in so many other places, beginners sometimes over-apply.

            • ❌ η σήμερα
            • ✅ σήμερα
            • ❌ το αύριο
            • ✅ αύριο

            "Today is Monday" = Σήμερα είναι Δευτέρα. No article before σήμερα.

            Exception: when an adverb is used as a noun-equivalent ("the today" = "today's news/edition"), Greek does add the article. But this is rare and you don't need it.

            Greek word order is more flexible than English. Adverbs can usually go in several positions:

            • Σήμερα δουλεύω. (At-the-start emphasis)
            • Δουλεύω σήμερα. (At-the-end, standard)
            • Δουλεύω πολύ σήμερα. (Mid-sentence, between verb and another modifier)

            Common patterns:

            • Time adverbs often start or end sentences: Αύριο φεύγω. / Φεύγω αύριο.
            • Intensifiers come right before what they modify: είμαι πολύ κουρασμένος
            • Frequency adverbs typically come right before the verb: Πάντα ξυπνάω νωρίς.
            1. Adverbs don't change. No agreement. Memorize the word once, use it forever.
            2. Most "how" adverbs come from adjectives by changing -ος → -α. καλός → καλά, γρήγορος → γρήγορα.
            3. NEVER put an article in front of an adverb. σήμερα, not η σήμερα.
            4. πολύ as a universal intensifier. πολύ κουρασμένος = very tired. Works on any adjective.
            5. Negation pairs. ποτέ / κανείς / τίποτα / πουθενά ALWAYS take δεν before the verb.
            6. Build a daily set: the 30-or-so adverbs in Part 3 cover ~90% of everyday conversation.

            → Drill in the 🎨 Modifiers chip in Practice

            Imperatives are the form you use to give commands or instructions ("Come!", "Wait!", "Tell me!"). In Greek they're a special mood of the verb with their own endings. Comprehension matters more than production at first — you'll hear imperatives constantly even if you rarely produce them.

            An imperative is a command, request, instruction, or invitation directed at someone. In English: Come! Wait. Sit down. Please pass the salt.

            Greek imperatives exist in only TWO forms — 2nd singular and 2nd plural. You can only command someone you're addressing directly; you can't issue an imperative to yourself or a third person (for that, Greek uses subjunctive with να).

            PersonUsed forExample
            2nd singular (informal)One person, friend/family/kidΈλα! (Come!)
            2nd plural (formal or many)Stranger, elder, OR groupΕλάτε! (Come! formal/plural)

            The most common pattern: take the aorist (perfective) stem and add -ε (singular) or -τε (plural).

            VerbAorist stemSingular imp.Plural imp.
            γράφω (write)γράψ-γράψε!γράψτε!
            τρώω (eat)φά-φάε!φάτε!
            πίνω (drink)πι-πιες!πιείτε!
            βλέπω (see)δ-δες!δείτε!
            λέω (say)πει-πες!πείτε!
            παίρνω (take)πάρ-πάρε!πάρτε!
            έρχομαι (come)irregularέλα!ελάτε!
            The connection to aspect: imperatives default to the perfective (one-time-action) form because you're usually commanding ONE specific instance. There's also an imperfective imperative for "do it repeatedly / habitually," but you can ignore it as a beginner.

            These are the imperatives Greeks use every day. Prioritize understanding over producing.

            GreekEnglishWhere you'll hear it
            Πάμε!Let's go!Universal "shall we?"
            Έλα!Come! / Come on!Server, host, parent
            Ελάτε!Come! (pl/formal)Restaurant: "ελάτε, ελάτε!"
            Πες μου.Tell me.Tutor, friend
            Δώσε μου.Give me.Anywhere
            Πάρε.Take it. / Here you go.Hand-off / offering
            Περίμενε.Wait.Service contexts
            Κοίτα.Look. / Listen here.Conversational lead-in
            Άκου.Listen.Conversational lead-in
            Σταμάτα.Stop.Traffic, kids
            Καλώς ήρθες!Welcome! (sg)Hospitality
            Καλώς ήρθατε!Welcome! (pl/formal)Shops, restaurants
            Πρόσεχε!Watch out! / Be careful!Warnings
            Κάθισε.Sit down.Restaurant host
            Φύγε!Go away! / Leave!Playful or angry

            To say "don't do X," Greek does not use the imperative form. It uses μην + subjunctive verb form.

            Don't (sg)Don't (pl)English
            Μη μιλάς!Μη μιλάτε!Don't talk!
            Μη φύγεις!Μη φύγετε!Don't leave!
            Μη το πεις.Μη το πείτε.Don't say it.
            Μην ανησυχείς.Μην ανησυχείτε.Don't worry.
            Μη φοβάσαι.Μη φοβάστε.Don't be afraid.
            Why μη and not δεν? δεν is for indicative statements ("I don't go"). μη(ν) is for the subjunctive / non-real mood. Imperatives, prohibitions, hypotheticals — all use μη(ν).

            μη vs μην: the ν is dropped before consonants in modern Greek, kept before vowels. Μη μιλάς (consonant after) vs Μην ανησυχείς (vowel after).

            Direct imperatives can sound blunt. To soften:

            (1) Use the plural/formal form: Δώστε μου (give me, formal) sounds politer than Δώσε μου.

            (2) Use a question with μπορείς να / μπορείτε να:

            • Μπορείτε να μου δώσετε τον λογαριασμό; — Can you give me the bill?
            • Μπορείς να με βοηθήσεις; — Can you help me?

            (3) Use θα ήθελα να ("I would like to"):

            • Θα ήθελα να δω το μενού. — I'd like to see the menu.
            • Θα ήθελα έναν καφέ, παρακαλώ. — I'd like a coffee, please.

            (4) Add παρακαλώ:

            • Πες μου, παρακαλώ. — Tell me, please.
            • Περίμενε λίγο, παρακαλώ. — Wait a moment, please.
            In professional and service contexts, default to μπορείτε να + verb. It's never wrong and always polite.
            1. Comprehension first, production second. Recognize the 15 daily imperatives in Part 3 — don't worry about producing them.
            2. Singular vs plural. Drill the pair: Έλα! / Ελάτε! — one informal, one formal.
            3. Negative = μη(ν) + subjunctive. NOT δεν. Drill Μη μιλάς / Μην ανησυχείς as fixed shapes.
            4. Polite version: μπορείς/μπορείτε να + verb. Universal request softener. Use it freely.
            5. παρακαλώ added to ANY command. Even a curt Πες μου becomes polite with , παρακαλώ tagged on.

            → Drill in the ❗ Imperative Drill (SRS) mode of Grammar Drill

            Translate between Greek and English instantly. Save translations directly to your vocab list.
